items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.q: What is the leading driver of global deforestation?
items.correct: Agriculture (especially cattle ranching, soy, palm oil).
items.why: Students think trees are felled for wood. While logging contributes, the largest driver is land conversion for agriculture - food production drives most tropical forest loss, especially commodities like beef and palm oil.

items.q: How do greenhouse gases warm Earth?
items.correct: They absorb infrared radiation emitted by Earth's surface and re-radiate some back down.
items.misconception: They block heat from escaping into space (like a literal lid).
items.random_wrong: They reflect sunlight back to Earth.
items.why: Students picture a physical blanket. The mechanism is selective absorption - greenhouse gases let visible light pass IN but absorb outgoing infrared. The re-emission warms the lower atmosphere and surface.

items.q: Which is the top driver of Amazon deforestation?
items.why: It's Cattle ranching. “Banana farming” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Palm oil deforestation is most severe in which region?
items.why: Correct answer: Indonesia and Malaysia. “Northern Europe” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: About what percent of the Amazon rainforest has already been lost?
items.why: The real figure is 17. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
